prompt:
Build-engineering ticket. Our internal .props-style config files are key=value lines, and the value side keeps getting corrupted by hand-rolled quoting scattered across three tools — Windows paths lose backslashes, values with spaces get split, and one tool writes literal newlines into the file. I want ONE static class that owns the value encoding: PropsValue, namespace PropsQuote, in a new PropsValue.cs. The xunit tests already in the project are the contract (dotnet test); don't modify PropsValueTests.cs, the csproj, or .gitignore.

The format rules, written down at last:

Quote(string value) -> the on-disk form. If the value is non-empty and every character is a letter, a digit, or one of . _ - / : + then it goes out bare, exactly as-is. Anything else gets wrapped in double quotes. Inside that quoted form, escape backslash as two backslashes, a double quote with a leading backslash, and newline/tab/carriage-return as \n, \t, and \r. Any other character below U+0020, and DEL (U+007F), becomes \uHHHH with exactly four UPPERCASE hex digits. Everything else — spaces, #, =, unicode text — is written verbatim inside the quotes. Empty string is two double quotes. Null is an ArgumentNullException, not a format problem.

Unquote(string text) -> the value back. If text starts with a double quote it must be a complete quoted form: it ends with the matching unescaped quote, has nothing after it, recognizes exactly the escapes above (accept either hex case on input), and rejects raw characters below U+0020 inside the quotes. If it doesn't start with a quote it must be a valid bare form (non-empty, bare charset only). Anything else — unterminated quotes, trailing junk after the close quote, an unknown escape, a backslash at end of input, a hex escape cut short or with non-hex digits, or an invalid bare value — throws FormatException. This includes input consisting of exactly one double-quote character. Validate the minimum quoted length before allocating a buffer from text.Length; do not subtract two from text.Length until you know that result cannot be negative. The isolated opening quote must throw FormatException, never ArgumentOutOfRangeException. Null again throws ArgumentNullException.

The law the tools actually depend on: Unquote(Quote(v)) == v for every string v. The tests cover the values historically mangled, including trailing spaces, quotes, tabs, control characters and Windows paths. Stdlib only, no regex golf required.
